data.title: Academy Sports and Outdoors Has Underappreciated Cash Flow Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With gross margin expansion, continued store expansion, healthy new store performance, and market share growth among shoppers earning over $100k per year, I see a path to impressive free cash flow growth for Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO). Trading at a TTM P/FCF of 11.1x, with what I believe to be depressed reported free cash flow from growth investments, store expansion, and a strategic inventory build, ASO might turn out to look very cheap in the years ahead. Any investment depends on the company continuing its steady store expansion and returning to top-line growth so that it can eventually begin re-accelerating free cash flow generation. Until then, the company is committed to buying back shares at relatively low prices, which could lead to even greater shareholder returns. I rate ASO a buy, and will continue to slowly accumulate shares.

Q1 Results

During Q1, ASO experienced a month-over-month sequential improvement in the business during Q1. The launch of the Jordan Brand across 145 stores, nearly half of ASO’s base, helped the company close with a relatively strong April. This could bode well for the trajectory of fiscal 2025, but tariff shock and inflationary risk still loom over the company, leading management to widen its FY 25 guide, reflecting the downside risk of a 145% China tariff scenario.

Positives for the quarter include a 60 bps gross margin expansion to 34.0% in Q1 25. SG&A expenses increased from 25.9% of sales to 28.8% of sales, deleveraging operating income margin by 240 bps, to 5.1%. The resulting net income margin contraction of 220 bps drove a decline in GAAP diluted EPS from $1.01 to $0.68 in Q1 25.

Diluted weighted average common shares outstanding declined 11.2% YoY, while end-of-Q1 diluted shares outstanding were down 9.2% YoY. ASO repurchased 1.87 million shares during Q1, and has a remaining share repurchase authorization of $536.5 million, as of May 3rd, 2025. The remaining authorized repurchase amount equals roughly 18.5% of ASO’s current market cap. Given the company’s track record of repurchases and strong cash flow generation, and ability to self-fund expansion, ASO is likely to utilize this program as a way to return capital to shareholders.

Cash Flows

There are some notable points to bring up about the cash flow statement. Firstly, net income declined by approximately $30 million, a clear headwind to operating cash flow. Secondly, ASO strategically increased inventory during Q1, with changes in inventory driving cash outflows of $251.2 million, versus 162.7 million in outflows during Q1 of 2024. That’s a nearly $90 million headwind to OCF relative to the prior year period. Meanwhile, Accounts Payable changes also grew during the quarter, YoY, with a $231.8 million adjustment on the cash flow statement, versus $186.5 million in the prior year.

The inventory build was about $90 million higher YoY, while the YoY increase in A/P was only about $45 million higher, resulting in a net incremental working capital outflow. The difference in just the inventory and A/P changes nets out to $43 million.

Going back to the Q4 24 call, the company mentioned that it planned to strategically stock up on "evergreen" inventory items, such as bicycles and free weights, in order to mitigate the impact of tariffs. This is exactly what it did during Q1. I believe this is a prudent approach to working capital management by the company, given the likelihood of higher inventory costs for products without seasonal demand risk or shelf life concerns. This is a temporary reduction in OCF this quarter, which reflects a strategic inventory decision and does not alter my view of ASO’s strong underlying cash flow generating potential.

Academy continues to showcase its ability to expand locations through a down cycle, while remaining highly profitable from a cash flow perspective. However, ASO will need to return to sales growth to continue driving net income growth and sustain long-term free cash flow generation.

Comp Sales, Guidance, And Key Drivers

Comp Sales and new stores are ultimately the key to re-accelerating NOPAT and FCF, and driving the investment thesis in ASO. While FY 25 guidance isn’t exactly inspiring, it offers hope that the bottom of the cycle for ASO is near.

When Academy last reported, it stated that the Q1 guide was “challenging” due to the company’s planned floor reset to clear space for its Jordan launch planned for the tail end of Q1 (April). The company highlighted key markdowns on underperforming items to clear shelf and floor space for the Jordan brand. Perhaps it’s not surprising to see that comp sales, which were trending in the right direction, QoQ, declined sequentially from -3% to -3.7%. Though comp sales improved YoY (from -5.7% in Q1 24), it’s not what I was looking for going into the report.

ASO’s guidance reflects the uncertainty of the 145% tariff on Chinese goods, as well as other reciprocal tariffs announced on April 2nd. The high end of its FY 2025 guidance remains largely unchanged, with only share count expectations and capital expenditures being adjusted downward, while EPS expectations were adjusted upwards due to buybacks. Low-end net sales are projected at $5.97 billion (down from $6.09b), and comp sales are projected at -4.0% (down from -2.0%). GAAP Net Income and EPS were reduced to $350 million and $5.10, respectively (down from $375m and $5.40), while adjusted Net Income and adjusted EPS were reduced to $375 million and $5.45 (down from $400m and $5.75). Low-end adjusted Free Cash Flow was reduced to $250 million (down from $290m). Adjusted FCF is defined as OCF minus Capex and Intangible purchases.

Below is the table provided in ASO’s press release, reflecting the adjusted guidance. I have highlighted positive changes in green, and negative revisions in red.

Thanks to expected buybacks, the high end of guidance for both GAAP and adjusted EPS was actually raised by $0.05, with diluted weighted average common shares outstanding guided at 69 million shares, down from the previous guidance of 70 million shares. Capital expenditures are expected to range between $180 and $220 million for FY 25.

Brand Expansion And Climbing The Income Ladder

The Jordan Brand is one potential driver of a return to growth. With only two weeks reported in Q1, and only half of ASO’s stores launching the Jordan Brand, the company is already seeing the brand outperform its expectations, leading the company to feel bullish about its prospects of becoming a top 10 brand. The Jordan brand was one of the most requested items on ASO’s website, so this could benefit both comp sales from existing stores, drive new traffic to both its existing and new stores, and gain market share among higher-income customers, traditionally not considered to be among its base shoppers.

Newness is an important driver in retail sales, particularly as customers become more discerning about shopping during a pullback in spending or worsening consumer sentiment. The Jordan brand may help inspire purchases that were previously unavailable to ASO, and even open the door to other brand expansions and introductions.

Adding premium brands might go hand-in-hand with ASO’s ability to maintain its growing share of customers earning $100,000 or more, which is its fastest-growing income demographic, and now represents roughly a third of its customer base. I think this could be a virtuous cycle. Higher earners are trading down into value retailers, like ASO, which might encourage premium brands, such as Hoka and On Holding, to open partnerships with ASO to sell some of its products. Last year, CEO Steve Lawrence stated that ASO was in talks to secure distribution from both Hoka and On, but that the two companies were having difficulty supplying existing retail partners and were unable to come to an agreement at the time. If ASO does acquire the ability to sell Hoka and On, as well as other premium apparel, sporting and recreation brands, it should support the company’s ability to maintain and expand share with higher income earners, supporting a broader sales opportunity and supporting new store expansion and healthy comp sales growth.

New Stores

Nothing has changed regarding ASO’s plans to open 20 – 25 new stores in 2025. Capex increased from $32.3 million in Q1 24 to $50.8 million in Q1 25. Management stated during the Q1 call, that most of its SG&A spending increases and CapEx is going towards growth investments in technology, distribution, and new store growth.

However, capital expenditures are expected to come in lower than initially anticipated, which appears to be the result of ASO delaying the signing of contracts for new stores planned for Q1 and Q2 of 2026. This poses a risk of a slowdown in expansion plans post-2025, that would eat into the growth potential for the company. The rationale provided by management, is that ASO is being cautious due to increased building costs. In the coming quarters, I will be looking for commentary suggesting that its 2026 and beyond expansion plans are firmly in place before believing this risk is behind us. Until then the fact that new stores continue to comp above base stores, and recent era new stores are expected to do the same.

If base stores begin to contribute to sales growth, from share gains with higher earning customers, from new brand launches, or e-commerce penetration, or a combination of all of these factors, then ASO should continue its past of generating increasing FCF per share.

Valuation

Determining a potential P/FCF multiple for a more mature ASO, with a higher FCF margin and overall revenue base is important in figuring out where the stock’s upside potential is.

I created a comp table, with key metrics, and valuation multiples. The green highlighted metrics show ASO, and some of its better comps. ASO’s 5 year average FCF margin of 8.7% is higher than Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS), its average GM is slightly lower than DKS’s but is expanding, and OM has some catching up to do. OCF growth lags the growth DKS has seen. DKS trades at a TTM P/FCF higher than ASO, which is currently 42.4x versus ASO’s 11.4x.

The P/FCF multiples in the table above have adjusted since the prior period. Looking at the company's current comps, ASO has historically traded at a steep discount to DKS, despite solid growth and an expanding store base.

If ASO sees its multiple close this gap at all, the company will see its stock price inflect higher.

Compared to its peer group, which includes Urban Outfitters (URBN), Gap (GAP), lululemon athletica (LULU), DKS, Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF), and American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), Academy undergenerates cash flows. ASO’s median FCF conversion from NOPAT is just 73.8% compared with the peer group’s 86.6% conversion rate. With a current 69.7% conversion rate, ASO could significantly close the gap between its free cash flow generation and its peers, justifying a potential re-rating of the stock. I believe that this conversion rate will fall within the range of its peers once the company's store expansion rate decreases relative to the company's size.

ASO generated $267.7 million in FCF over the TTM period. If it were already converting 86.6% of NOPAT to FCF, its TTM FCF would be $332.4 million instead, and the stock would be trading at a P/FCF multiple of 9.2x, rather than 11.4x.

If over the next five years, ASO grows NOPAT at 8% annually, with a conversion rate of 86.6% to FCF, the company would generate $488.7 million in FCF by year five. If the stock earns a re-rating to 13x FCF, the stock price would equate to approximately $95.58 per share without buybacks, good for a 17.0% CAGR; and $112.45 per share if ASO reduces its share count by 15%, good for a 20.9% CAGR.

If ASO only grows NOPAT at 4% annually, with a lower conversion rate of 75.0% to FCF, the company would generate $350.5 million in FCF by year five. In this case, if the stock is only granted a multiple of 9.5x FCF, the stock price would equate to approximately $50.09 per share without buybacks, good for a paltry 2.8% CAGR; and $58.93 per share if ASO reduces its share count by 15%, just a 6.2% CAGR.

ASO can grow NOPAT and FCF in this range by continuing its successful store expansion, increasing its share with higher-income earners, and capturing higher-margin, premium apparel, equipment, and footwear brands, which the Jordan Brand expansion may help the company achieve.

Risks

Risks include a period of prolonged macroeconomic uncertainty, tariffs impacting margins and/or sales, and a slower-than-projected store expansion.

Tariffs threaten both sales from price-sensitive consumers and might cut into margins if ASO is unable to attain concessions from suppliers or mitigate inventory dollar increases and COGS inflation from supply chain adjustments.

If ASO faces more than simple lease signing delays for its planned 2026 new store expansion, the company may lose some of its growth potential and have the market downgrade the stock even further.

Conclusion

ASO is currently a cheap stock, is buying back large amounts of shares, and sitting on the potential for increased free cash flow generation. If and when the tide turns, sales growth returns, and the company's investments in growth become a smaller portion of NOPAT and operating cash flow, ASO will see a meaningful acceleration in reported free cash flow. This would likely drive a significant positive re-rating of the stock. Any investor in the company might be best served by remaining patient, as sentiment for this company may take several quarters or longer to improve. I rate ASO a buy, and plan to slowly accumulate shares before the tide turns.

Whenever I rate a stock a buy, I commit to purchasing shares in the company. For full disclosure, I added to my existing position in ASO 72 hours prior to submitting this article and plan to add again 72 hours following publication of this article.

data.title: Definitive Healthcare Q1 Review: Revenue Slumps, Structural Issues Surface, And A SELL Rating Sticks
data.description:
Definitive Healthcare Corp. (DH), an industry leader in healthcare commercial intelligence, last month reported not-so-inspiring first-quarter (Q1 2025) financial results which saw a 7% revenue decline year-on-year to $59.2 million. Although management appears happy with the performance as sales were $3 million above its prior guidance, I wouldn’t touch DH stock right now and would rate it a SELL as the company continues to suffer revenue losses despite sustained investments in sales and marketing functions.

Definitive Healthcare provides data-driven healthcare commercial intelligence, enabling clients to identify key markets, opportunities, and stakeholders through a powerful SaaS platform that drives strategic growth and success. Its revenue is declining in 2025, and sales recently touched their lowest level in nine quarters in March. I believe this revenue decline is not only temporary but structural, potentially stemming from customer dissatisfaction. The company also provided a lacklustre Q2 2025 revenue guidance in the range of $58.5 – $60.0 million, and I’m afraid, sales could keep sliding.

DH In a Structural Decline: A Story Minted In A COVID-19 Era Crisis

Definitive Healthcare was once a high-flying stock. The company first listed in Sept 2021 at an IPO price of $27 per share, offering 15.6 million shares whilst raising around $420 million. A day after its listing, its share price rose to as high as $48.99. The stock has been in a free fall since then. Down 90% in four years, DH shares trade near their all-time lows around $3.66, and the sustained fall from the market’s grace is disturbing, yet with no rebound opportunity in sight.

The healthcare technology company’s listing during the COVID-19 pandemic was perfectly timed, but the regime has changed. The pandemic created market conditions that both increased the relevance of and made the IPO market more receptive to companies offering healthcare intelligence and data services, and Definitive Healthcare was well received.

It is possible that the company leveraged the market’s momentum and positive investor sentiment on industry stocks to go public at a valuation that may not have been possible in a different environment. At IPO, Definitive was richly valued with Price to Sales (at 16) and EV/EBITDA (at 46) multiples reflecting strong growth optimism. However, the market has corrected. The COVID-ear sentiment is gone, industry stocks fetch normalized multiples, and DH stock has to perform on the revenue, earnings, and cash flow generation front to impress its investor base. Yet, the company could be structurally in a decline loop.

The pandemic exposed major gaps in North American public health infrastructure, care coordination, and provider visibility. That accelerated demand for healthcare market data offerings, similar to Definitive Healthcare’s offerings, as all sectoral stakeholders needed real-time intelligence on providers, facilities, and treatment trends. The pandemic triggered a surge of interest in digital health, SaaS platforms, and companies enabling data-driven decisions. Customers are increasingly choosy as digital healthcare, SaaS platforms, and market intelligence and analytics offerings mature and evolve. Investor sentiment in the industry has cooled off, and only the best industry players retain investor enthusiasm. DH is failing to retain its customer base, and investors are losing interest in the stock. Yet, some peers like WayStar (WAY) are thriving.

Why Is Definitive Healthcare Struggling To Grow Revenue?

The company primarily generates revenue through its subscription-based data platforms such as View, Monocl, and Carevoyance, which offer in-depth insights into healthcare providers, organizational affiliations, and claims data. A substantial portion of its income comes from enterprise clients, defined as those contributing over $100,000 in annual recurring revenue. These large customers’ number declined to 519 by the end of 2024, from 529 in 2023. In addition to these core offerings, the company provides professional services designed to enhance and support the use of its data solutions. While exact revenue breakdowns are undisclosed, subscriptions clearly remain the dominant source of income.

In 2024, Definitive experienced notable headwinds in customer retention and enterprise client engagement, signalling deeper structural challenges. The decline in net dollar retention to 90% for enterprise customers and 85% overall suggests the company struggled not only to maintain existing client relationships but also to upsell or cross-sell effectively within its current client base. Net dollar revenue retention remained a core weakness in Q1 2025 as it remained well below its historic target of above 100%. I believe this metric is a key indicator of customer satisfaction and product value, as it reflects potential issues such as unmet expectations, increasing competition, or insufficient integration into client workflows.

Compounding this concern, the enterprise customer count which dropped by 21 in Q4 ending the year at 519, points to the loss of high-value accounts that are typically more stable and strategically important.

Together, lower revenue retention and declining customer count raise concerns about the company's ability to sustain predictable recurring revenue and highlight the urgency for improved customer success strategies, deeper product integration, and clearer return on investment (ROI) delivery.

In my view, challenges of this nature could mean two things; Either the DH systems were not so user-friendly and did not match the competition, or customer care was not so good. The company responded by investing in master data management capabilities in early 2022 (after acquiring Analytical Wizards) aimed to improve data quality and integration into customer workflows. The market responded positively, as evidenced by improved data hygiene, streamlined user workflows, favourable client and analyst feedback, and one company adding 40,000 executive contacts through enhanced integration.

Unyielding Sales And Marketing Efforts

Since listing, DH has consistently reported quarterly losses. Quarterly revenue since Q1 2023 to Q4 2024 has been in a tight range of $59.2 million and $65.9 million (the best quarter being Q4 2023). For the most recent quarter, sales revenue dropped to its lowest point at $59.2 million, whilst sales and marketing expense remained higher at $20.65 million.

Higher marketing expenses and sales efforts aren’t yielding expected revenue growth. That, together with a drop in customer count, is a clear sign that something is wrong within the company’s product mix.

Definitive Healthcare Succumbing To Stronger Competition?

I believe that close competitors Waystar, IQVIA, Clarify Health, and Innovaccer could be the main players taking revenue away from Definitive Healthcare. Waystar specializes in revenue cycle management and has integrated analytics that improve financial performance for healthcare providers. Their strong customer engagement and retention strategies have positioned them well in the market. Unlike DH, Waystar’s Q1 2025 revenue rose 14% YoY, driven by strong AI integration, high client retention, and expanding subscription revenues.

On the other hand, IQVIA is offering large-scale, diversified healthcare analytics with a strong global reach and deeper integration across pharma, providers, and payers - something DH is struggling with. Clarify Health and Innovaccer, provide cloud-native platforms with real-time analytics, predictive modelling, and seemingly better Electronic Health Record (EHR) integration, giving them an edge in workflow efficiency and decision-making over Definitive Healthcare. I believe these well-endowed competitors are winning because they align their value propositions more closely with evolving client needs, deliver measurable ROI, and offer a better user experience. Additionally, their stronger customer success and retention strategies, as reflected in higher Net Promoter Scores (NPS), are potentially allowing them to capture and hold the market share DH is losing.

I believe DH’s poor customer retention is attributable to a couple of reasons, including product complexity. Its platforms such as View and Monocl may not be user-friendly or as seamlessly integrated into client workflows as competitor offerings, leading to dissatisfaction. Also, in a crowded market, DH’s offerings may be failing to stand out in terms of features or measurable ROI compared to competitors. The decline in net dollar retention might suggest that existing clients are not finding sufficient value to stay engaged or expand their usage.

Respectable Cashflow, Balance Sheet Concerns

Definitive Healthcare continues to generate positive cash flow from operations that should sustain the business. It reported $26.1 million cash flow from operations and $22.9 million in unlevered free cash flow for Q1 2025. While this indicates strong liquidity and operational sustainability, the balance sheet revealed some notable concerns. While cash and cash equivalents remained stable at about $106 million, short-term investments declined sharply from $184.8 million to $94.6 million, suggesting a drawdown on reserves. The company also posted a significant net loss of $155.1 million, driven largely by a $176.5 million goodwill impairment.

Total assets have dropped from $1.09 billion to $808.7 million. The most prominent concern in my opinion is the company's substantial goodwill and intangible assets, which, while not precisely broken out, appear to comprise a significant portion of total assets (a total of $501.5 million or 46% of the balance sheet).

Shareholders’ equity decreased from $607.2 million to $435.9 million.

Despite these setbacks, the company’s low current liabilities relative to current assets offer some liquidity cushion, supported by respectable cash generation.

I believe equity investors in DH’s stock are not in immediate danger, but the large impairment charges, equity erosion, and a reliance on adjustments to show profitability do raise sustainability red flags. Continued operational execution and preservation of cash flow will be essential to maintain investor confidence.

Beware: DH’s Concerning Goodwill Impairment

Definitive Healthcare started providing for goodwill impairment in Q3 2023 ($287.4 million) and has continued doing so, a trend which could reflect a dying business. The latest results are showing a goodwill impairment figure of $176.5 million in Q4 2024.

These goodwill impairments, combined with recent large client losses, could imply technological obsolescence or major market share loss to competition. Goodwill impairments usually come about when management is no longer confident that an acquired business or asset will produce the initially expected cash flows when it was acquired, and DH’s impairments imply that all is not well.

Valuation

Definitive Healthcare’s EV/EBITDA ratio of 11.02 is notably lower than the sector median of 13.60, indicating that the company is trading at a relatively more attractive valuation compared to the average in its industry.

Compared to its direct competitors, DH’s valuation appears conservative. Change Healthcare’s EV/EBITDA of 19.4 suggests it is priced at a significant premium, likely reflecting higher growth expectations, stronger profitability, or a more diversified business model. IQVIA’s comparable EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.74 is higher than DH’s but closer to the sector median, suggesting a moderate premium that may be justified by its larger scale or market positioning. Overall, DH appears to offer better value relative to these peers, which could appeal to contrarian investors looking for exposure to healthcare data analytics at a lower relative price.

However, I'm of the opinion that this valuation accurately reflects the company's current struggles, as there is no clear sign of a near-term revenue turnaround.

Conclusion: Avoiding DH Stock In June

Definitive Healthcare stock faces significant challenges in my view. These include a sustained and structural revenue decline, loss of key enterprise clients, ongoing quarterly losses, and substantial goodwill impairments signalling deeper issues. Despite a lower valuation compared to peers, DH’s cheap price highlights the business’s lack of clear a recovery path and poor growth prospects. Given these factors, I’d avoid the healthcare technology stock for now, and advise investors (especially the medium to long-term oriented holders) to SELL DH stock to mitigate the risk of further value erosion.

data.title: Wake Up Wall Street - 2 Massively Mispriced Dividend Gems
data.description:
Introduction

"Is the bottom in?"

I may be mistaken, as I don't have scientific research to back it up, but I'm pretty sure this is one of the most common questions on people's minds after a market sell-off.

Essentially, it's entirely based on an even more straightforward question: "Will stocks go up from now on (with less drama)?"

Technically speaking, answering this question with “yes” is the safest bet.

Historically speaking, stocks always go up. We just need to give it time.

One of my favorite charts (the one below) shows that on a 20-year rolling basis since 1950 (it also works going much further back), investors who put 100% of their capital in stocks never had a losing track record. In fact, even on a five-year basis, the odds of negative returns were very close to zero.

Investors who maintained a 60/40 equity/bonds portfolio never lost money on a five-year rolling period since 1950, with annual returns somewhere between bonds and stocks, which obviously makes sense from a mathematical point of view.

With that said, going back to the main question, if you were to ask analysts, they would probably tell you a bottom is in.

Why?

That's based on a so-called "bottom-up" approach, which takes the price target of every single S&P 500 stock into account. In other words, it's different from the usual S&P 500 price target, which people usually refer to.

While I am extremely skeptical when it comes to any analyst estimates, I think it's interesting to mention that analysts are more upbeat for the first time since President Trump's 'Liberation Day' press conference.

I'm bringing all of this up for a few reasons.

  • I always like to bring up new stats and charts that may be of interest.
  • Even more important, Bloomberg's Jonathan Levin wrote that these upgrades aren't just caused by Big Tech but by a wide range of companies. This supports my growth-to-value and market-broadening theses, which I have maintained for a few years, and discussed in articles like this one.

Here's one of my favorite quotes, as I am bullish on all companies mentioned and because it reinforces that there is a lot of “reliable value” in areas that are not Big Tech.

But even industrial firms, which you’d expect to be more sensitive to trade volatility, have garnered renewed Wall Street optimism. Analysts have sharply revised up price targets for the likes of Boeing Co., GE Aerospace, and Deere & Co., reinforcing how their size gives them negotiating power and how there’s always more going on beyond just White House policy and macroeconomics. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

Obviously, all of this needs to be taken with a grain of salt. After all, analysts do not have crystal balls. They also tend to have a bullish bias, which includes hiking targets after a rally has occurred. As we only benefit from forward-looking statements, that is hardly something to rely on to generate alpha.

The good news, however, is that when using a "bottom-up" approach is that we only include data about single companies, which are usually covered by analysts who have a good understanding of these businesses. That's why these predictions can be "self-fulfilling prophecies," as Mr. Levine writes.

I added emphasis to the quote below.

In 21 years of data, there are only eight other instances in which the aggregate bottom-up price target declined by 3% or more (early 2025 was the ninth). In seven of them, a 1% recovery in the target price meant that the bottom was in (though the onset of Covid undid the 2019 rally). The median forward return for the S&P 500 was 4.8%, 6.5%, and 15% three, six, and 12 months later, respectively. - Bloomberg

Essentially, analysts are not bad at all at predicting the bottom when using a bottom-up approach, which may also be because stocks, in general, tend to rise over time.

All things considered, while this is still a very annoying market due to headline risks, macroeconomic challenges, and valuation headwinds, I believe my thesis continues to be confirmed, which is that the future is bright (it always is) and there is still a lot of value to be found in areas that may be overlooked by many.

That's why I'm dedicating the second part of this article to two standout dividend stocks that I expect to deliver consistently rising income with low drama, thanks to fantastic business models.

  • One is a higher-yielding company that has a unique blend of income, growth, and safety.
  • The other is a lower-yielding dividend grower with high growth, defensive demand, and a lot of secular growth.

With A 5% Yield, ONEOK (OKE) Remains A Strong Buy With Secular Growth

Oil isn't doing so well right now. Excluding the spike caused by the attack on Iran, of course.

While WTI has recovered substantially from its lows, a price in the mid-$60 range is bad news for American drillers, as it makes it very likely that a lack of new investments will cause oil production to decline.

Anything below $70 to $75 WTI on a longer-term basis (ignoring short-term spikes) will likely pressure total output, according to the Dallas Fed Energy Survey and most of the companies that I monitor on a frequent basis.

This has dragged down ONEOK, which is one of America's largest midstream companies, which is ironic because the company has no direct exposure to oil prices due to the absence of any production.

Like its peers, it owns midstream assets. It owns a lot of them, as its asset base includes a 60,000-mile pipeline network, as well as gathering, fractionation, and storage assets that cover natural gas, refined products, and crude oil.

As the map above shows, ONEOK operates mainly in the mid-continent region, extending from the Canadian border to the Gulf Coast, with key operational areas in the Bakken, Permian Basin, Mid-Continent, and Gulf Coast regions.

In other words, ONEOK is extremely "mission-critical."

It's also extremely resilient. While lower oil output would be bad news for the U.S. energy sector, it would not be *that* bad for ONEOK, as the overwhelming majority of its EBITDA is generated in fast-growing natural gas liquids (NGL), natural gas, and gathering and processing operations.

As we can see below, even during the pandemic, when volumes fell by 20%, the company managed to grow its adjusted EBITDA. This year, it is still guiding for $8.2 billion in EBITDA, with more than 15% EPS growth and 10% higher EBITDA in the year after that.

It also helps that OKE is in a fantastic spot to benefit from more growth in the NGL market, as it handles ethane, propane, isobutane, butane, and natural gasoline. These byproducts are high-margin products that enjoy tremendous growth overseas, especially for industrial uses and energy demand in emerging markets.

ONEOK operates both mixed NGL pipelines and purity product pipelines, as well as an LPG export facility in Texas City with a capacity of 400,000 barrels per day in a partnership with MPLX (MPLX), which is another midstream company I like.

Products like ethane are relatively cheap, which means even tariffs are unlikely to cause demand destruction.

And, in order to grow, the company focuses on brownfield opportunities, which means investments in underused assets or existing facilities that can be expanded. This is much more efficient than building new assets from scratch.

It also helps that the company has engaged in major M&A in recent years, which has tremendously increased its existing footprint, making it easier to compete, while providing it with an advantage to invest in future growth.

In general, the company's capital allocation strategy prioritizes organic growth projects, dividend stability, and maintaining a target debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.5x, which is all good news for investors.

Moreover, with regard to stability, the company has sustained its dividend through very challenging periods, including 2020. Both the EBITDA growth chart above and the dividend chart below display this stability.

Currently yielding 5.1%, the company targets 3-4% annual dividend growth.

It also has a $2 billion stock buyback program in place, although its use has been limited due to attractive growth opportunities. Looking ahead, excess cash is expected to support buybacks or enhanced dividends if growth projects diminish.

All things considered, the company's double-digit expected EBITDA/operating cash flow growth and its dividend pave the way for double-digit annual returns.

This is why I expect current headwinds in energy to provide fantastic buying opportunities in OKE.

It's also why I will continue discussing OKE, as I have a very hard time finding better opportunities than this for investors seeking both income and growth.

On a side note, I am bullish on oil as well, as I wrote in an in-depth article in April. I will likely write an update soon.

The Cigna Group (CI) - Forget UnitedHealth, Cigna Has It All

Healthcare has become a bit of a minefield. Even one of the best compounders ever, UnitedHealth Group (UNH), has been punished severely, as sticky inflation and high secular growth in healthcare demand have pressured its stock price.

It forced me to write an article titled "UNHappy Ending: How Big Challenges Nuked My UnitedHealth Thesis," which was still bullish, by the way, as I like the value UNH offers after its crash.

However, this isn't about UnitedHealth. This is about Cigna Group, a healthcare giant that has been able to avoid most of UnitedHealth's troubles thanks to a more bulletproof business model, which is based on two growth platforms called Evernorth, its health services portfolio, and Cigna Healthcare, which is its integrated benefits portfolio.

Unlike UnitedHealth, Cigna has a stronger focus on pharmacy benefit management and less emphasis on direct care delivery, which protects it against elevated inflation and risks in direct care, a huge benefit in this market.

Its pharmacy benefit services cater to more than 100 million Americans and processed north of 2 billion prescriptions in 2024, with roughly 90% coming from cost-effective generics. Also, this segment saved clients $38 billion in 2024 through negotiated lower prices for branded drugs and high generic utilization.

This is good for growth.

For example, in Q1'25, Evernorth, which covers 60% of total earnings and specialty care services on top of pharmacy benefit services, saw 5% year-over-year growth. Adjusted pre-tax earnings were up by 11%.

Its Cigna business is also doing well, despite high expenses in areas like surgical and OB services. In the first quarter of this year, revenue rose by 9%, supported by U.S. employers and International Health. As its market share is just 7%, the company is very upbeat about growth in a fragmented market.

In general, Cigna is bullish, as 13% annual EPS growth between 2014 and 2024 is expected to be followed by 6-9% annual earnings growth in the future, with most of this coming from the specialty and healthcare businesses.

This is expected to lead to maintaining the company's history of double-digit earnings per share growth, as buybacks and M&A are expected to lead to another 4-5% annual benefit on a per-share basis.

When adding its dividend of currently 1.9%, we get a total return potential of up to 16%, which would be based on 10-14% annual EPS growth and up to 2% of dividend income. In Q1'25, its targets were confirmed, which is great news in light of UnitedHealth's turmoil.

Please note that the total return is EPS growth based on unchanged valuation multiples in addition to dividends. In other words, if a stock always maintains the same valuation multiple, its stock price performance will be equal to earnings growth.

Before I tell you what this means for the valuation, I want to bring up that the company's capital plans include $60 billion in spending, with up to 65% of this being earmarked for strategic M&A, buybacks, and debt repayment. That's how serious this company is about growing the per-share value of the business.

The dividend will account for just 15% of its capital.

That's a good deal, as the company requires minimal effort to sustain its 1.9% yield. This dividend has a 3-year CAGR of 11.1%, a mile above the industry median of 6.2%.

In other words, although a 1.9% yield is not particularly high, it is supported by significant long-term growth potential, which is likely to result in high yields on cost for long-term investors.

With regard to its valuation, the story gets better.

As we just discussed, the company has double-digit annual total return potential if it is able to execute its growth plans. The cherry on top is the fact that CI shares trade at just 10.9x earnings, below their long-term average of 12.1x, and supported by analyst expectations of 12% annual EPS growth after 2025, in line with guidance.

While current economic and market headwinds could keep high-quality stocks cheap, there's a strong case for a theoretical annual return of at least 14-15% per year going forward, making CI one of the best dividend growth stocks on my radar.

Takeaway

So, is the bottom in?

Statistically speaking, the odds are in our favor, as stocks tend to rise over time.

As we discussed in the intro, analysts' bottom-up price targets suggest optimism. While everything analysts say needs to be taken with a grain of salt, the upgrades beyond Big Tech and broadening growth expectations align with my thesis, which is that there's a lot of value to be found in "less popular" places.

That said, instead of obsessing over short-term noise, I'm focusing on durable opportunities like I always do.

This includes ONEOK, a 5%-yielding midstream powerhouse with secular growth, which I cannot cover enough at current valuations.

Then there's Cigna, a healthcare gem I haven't paid much attention to in recent years, which offers double-digit return potential, mainly driven by elevated EPS growth.

All things considered, I believe both offer the kind of resilience and upside that make market dips worth dealing with.

So, what's the bottom line?

The future is bright (it always is), but the real wins come from sticking to quality, ignoring the drama, and letting time do the heavy lifting.

Just like the Billy Bass Fish!

Risks To My Thesis

One of the biggest risks to my thesis is an environment of very subdued oil prices, which could pressure oil production in the U.S. However, even in 2020, ONEOK has proven to be resistant to these stocks.

Furthermore, Cigna could be hurt by elevated claims, inflation pressuring healthcare costs, and/or an inability to achieve its high-growth targets.

Also, general market risks apply, as any bigger drawdown could temporarily hurt these stocks.
